The airlock cycles. A woman in an unmarked flight suit takes the compass from your hands with a nod — no speeches, no ceremony. She holds it the way a navigator holds a chart: with professional care and no sentiment.

You watch the compass go without regret. The silver veins pulse once against the woman's gloved palm, then settle. It knows it is going somewhere useful.

The unmarked ship breaks away and jumps. You and Voss sit in the cockpit in the silence that follows. The nav console is empty. Your hands are light.

'Feel strange?' Voss asks.

'No,' you say. And mean it.

Some things are worth more than what you can sell them for. You and Voss fly on, lighter and better, into a galaxy that just got a little more complicated for the Empire.

You survived. You found the artefact. And you gave it away.

That might be the best thing you have ever done.

end_resistance_keep.text:
You keep the compass — but you share the coordinates. Everything the compass showed you, every frequency, every jump point to the dead star and the signal beyond it. The unmarked ship records it all.

A compromise. The cause gets what it needs. You keep what you found.

Voss watches the data transfer complete and smiles — tight, tired, real. 'Good enough,' he says.

The unmarked ship breaks away and jumps. You sit in the cockpit with the compass warm in your hand, its silver veins pulsing with that ancient, patient light. The coordinates are shared now. The Empire's monopoly on Precursor knowledge cracked open by a scavenger, a deserter, and a woman who ran a tea stall.

You engage the drive. The frontier awaits. And for the first time in a long time, the word 'frontier' feels like it means something.

You survived. You kept the artefact. And you did not keep it to yourself.

That will have to be enough. It is.

end_survivor.text:
Voss finds you in the cockpit as the FTL drive spools up. He has a fresh bandage on his arm and a look on his face that you have not seen before — something close to peace.

'Where are we going?' he asks.

'Anywhere that isn't here.'

He settles into the co-pilot's seat like someone who has needed a co-pilot's seat for a long time. His hands find the console controls with the ease of old training — Imperial Navy, you remember. Before everything went wrong.

You engage the drive. Stars stretch to lines. The Sable Moth leaps into the void.

In your hand, the Void Compass pulses — warm, alive, ancient. It contains star charts to places no living being has visited in ten thousand years. Precursor worlds. Lost civilisations. The kind of discoveries that change the course of history.

But that is a story for another day. Today, you survived. You found the artefact. You made an ally. And somewhere behind you, the Empire is learning that not everything in the galaxy can be taken by force.

You lean back in the pilot's chair and watch the stars burn. Beside you, Voss does the same.

end_lone_wolf.text:
You engage the FTL drive alone. Stars stretch to lines. The Sable Moth shudders once, then leaps into the void, leaving New Babylon Station as a dwindling point of light in the rear viewport.

In your hand, the Void Compass pulses — warm, alive, ancient. It contains star charts to places no living being has visited in ten thousand years. Precursor worlds. Lost civilisations. Treasure beyond counting.

You are alone, as always. But you are alive, and you are rich, and the Empire can choke on its own arrogance.

You set a course for the frontier. There is a fence on Tellus IV who deals in Precursor artefacts and does not ask questions. After that — well. The Compass is a map. And you have always wondered what lies beyond the edge of known space.

The stars burn cold and bright. The void awaits.

end_pyrrhic.text:
You escaped. You are alive. The station recedes in the viewport, lit up with Imperial activity — searchlights sweeping the hull, patrol craft launching from the docking ring, comm channels crackling with cordon orders. They are tearing through every deck looking for you, and they will find evidence of everything you did down there. The dead soldiers. The disrupted sensors. The trail of a scavenger who fought instead of running.

Malachar will not forget this. The Bureau does not forget. Your face will be in their files by morning, your ship's signature flagged at every port from here to the Core.

The Void Compass pulses in your hand. You have what you came for. But the person who ran the diversion that bought you your exit is not on this ship. Voss. You do not know if he made it out, or if he is in Imperial custody, or if the station took him the way it takes everything else eventually.

You set a course. The stars burn cold and indifferent. You survived. That used to be enough.

It is harder to be sure of that now.

death_extraction.text:
You reached Voss. That was the easy part. Getting out again — that was the part you did not plan for.

The cordon closed behind you. Three soldiers became six. Six became a full squad. They came from both ends of the corridor, cutting off the junction, and the mathematics of the situation became very simple.

Voss fought beside you. He was good — better than you expected, clean shots, disciplined movement. But there were too many of them, and the corridor was too narrow, and eventually a stun round found him and then one found you.

The Void Compass rolls from your hand as you fall. Its silver veins pulse once, twice, then go dark.

Malachar arrives three minutes later. He picks up the compass without looking at either of you. An aide takes notes.

Your adventure ends here.

e_pre_1.text:
You follow the waypoint left, but after thirty metres the corridor changes.

The frost stops. Not gradually — it ends in a clean line across the floor, as if the cold knows better than to go further. Beyond that line the walls are smooth dark metal, seamless, without bolt or weld. The ceiling is higher than it should be. The proportions are wrong in a way you can't immediately name.

Your boots echo differently here. The sound doesn't bounce — it drops, swallowed by surfaces that weren't designed for human acoustics.

There is a side passage to your left. It has no door. The opening is shaped like a tall, narrow oval, its edges bevelled inward. Through it, faintly, you can see light. Not station light. Something cooler, steadier, with no visible source.

choices.text: Step through the oval passage toward the light
Stay on course — follow the waypoint to Research Lab 7

e_pre_2.text:
The passage is short — four steps — but the proportions make it feel longer. The oval opening is wide enough for you to walk through without turning sideways, but only just. It was sized for something narrower at the shoulder and taller at the crown.

You emerge into a room.

It is not large. Roughly octagonal, perhaps ten metres across, though the angles are not quite regular — some walls are slightly longer than others, giving the space an asymmetry that feels deliberate. The floor is the same dark metal, but here it slopes. Not toward a wall. Toward the centre, where a shallow depression holds a drain or channel of some kind. The channel leads down at an angle that does not correspond to the station's gravity.

The light comes from the ceiling. No fixture, no panel. The metal itself holds a faint luminescence, blue-white, constant. It has been doing this for a very long time. You are certain of that without knowing why.

Against the far wall, there are recesses. Shallow niches set into the metal at varying heights. Some are at chest level. Others are near the floor. Their shapes suggest they once held objects, but the objects are gone. The niches have too many curves — sized for things with geometries your hands wouldn't know how to grip.

choices.text: Examine the recesses more closely
Follow the channel in the floor — see where it leads

e_pre_3.text:
You crouch beside one of the lower niches and hold your hand inside it. The surface is warm. Not from any mechanism you can detect — no vibration, no hum. The warmth is in the metal itself, as though it remembers being held.

The niche's shape is complex. Five lobes, but they branch at the tips, and the branches fork again. Whatever rested here had a structure more like coral than bone. You try to imagine the hand — the limb — the body that would reach for this shelf at this height, at this angle, and your mind slides off the picture.

In the niche beside it, higher up, you find something. A thin disc of the same dark metal, no wider than your palm. It is lodged in the back of the recess as though it slipped behind whatever was meant to sit there. You pry it loose.

It weighs almost nothing. One face is smooth. The other has a pattern of raised lines — not writing, not circuitry. Something between the two. The lines converge toward one edge of the disc, all of them, like rivers flowing toward a coast you can't see.

choices.text: Follow the channel in the floor before you leave
Return to the main corridor and head for Research Lab 7

e_pre_4.text:
The channel in the floor is shallow — barely deep enough to hold water, if water is what it was meant to hold. It runs from the central depression to the far wall, where it passes through a slit in the metal and continues beyond.

You follow it to the wall and kneel. Through the slit, you can see the channel continuing — sloping down, curving left, descending into the station's deeper structure. Or into whatever the station was built on top of.

The light is stronger here. You press your face close to the slit and look.

Below — far below, further than the station's hull should allow — there is a space. You can't judge the size. The light from the channel illuminates only the nearest surfaces: more dark metal, more curves, more geometry that doesn't repeat. But the sense of volume is enormous. The air that comes through the slit is dry and cold and carries no smell at all.

You are looking at the top of something. Whatever this room is, it is the uppermost point of a structure that extends downward into a space the station maps don't show.

The light below is not diffuse. It comes from a direction. You turn your head and the angle resolves: it comes from galactic north-east. From a point in the sky that, at this station's position, holds nothing charted.

choices.text: Examine the wall recesses before you leave
You've seen enough — return to the corridor and find Research Lab 7

e_pre_5.text:
You step back through the oval passage into the main corridor. The frost is waiting on the other side of that clean line. Station light. Station sounds. The ticking of thermal expansion in the hull plates.

You stand still for a moment. The waypoint arrow on your data chip is patient, pointing ahead toward Research Lab 7. The Void Compass is close.

But you are thinking about the light. The direction it came from. The way every line in that room pointed the same way, like a compass needle that was never meant to spin.

choices.text: Continue to Research Lab 7

b_wit_1.text:
You don't take the direct route. Instead you move along the container stacks, keeping low, working your way through the maze of freight modules toward the far side of the bay. Longer path. Better cover.

You hear them before you see them. Boots on deck plating — not running, not hurried. A measured pace. The sound of people who have a schedule.

You press yourself into the gap between two shipping containers and look through the slit where their edges don't quite meet.

Three soldiers in Bureau grey. Standard sidearms, holstered. They're not expecting a fight. Between them, a small group — a woman in maintenance coveralls, an older man with a station tech badge, a kid who can't be more than sixteen. Residents. The kind of people who live in cargo bays because they can't afford the upper decks.

The soldiers are walking them toward the freight elevator at the far end of the bay. Nobody is in restraints. Nobody is being dragged. They're just walking.

choices.text: Keep watching

b_wit_2.text:
The woman in coveralls stops. She turns to the nearest soldier — a young man, clean-shaven, unremarkable.

'We haven't done anything,' she says. Not shouting. Not pleading. Just stating it, the way you'd correct a clerical error. 'We live here. We work Bay 14 cargo sorting. We don't know anything about Precursor artefacts.'

The soldier looks at her. He nods, once, the way you'd acknowledge someone telling you the time. Then he puts his hand on her shoulder — not roughly — and guides her forward. She goes. There is nothing else to do.

The older man says something you can't hear. The soldier behind him answers. You catch the tail end of it: '...processing area. Shouldn't take long.'

Processing. That's the word. Not detention, not arrest. Processing. Like cargo. Like inventory being moved to a different bay before audit.

The kid is the last one through the elevator doors. He looks back over his shoulder, scanning the cargo bay — not for rescue, just looking at the place where he lives, as if he is trying to remember it. The doors close.

The Void Compass sits in your pack. You are aware of its weight in a way you weren't before.

choices.text: There's nothing you can do here. Move on.
Follow the soldiers toward the freight elevator

b_wit_3.text:
You move to the freight elevator. The indicator light shows it descending — sub-level 2, sub-level 3. It stops at sub-level 4. A storage tier. Nothing down there but empty containers and station infrastructure.

Nothing down there that anyone would need to see.

You stand at the elevator doors for a moment longer than you should. You could call it back. Ride it down. Find them. And then what. Fight three armed soldiers. Rescue three people you don't know to a station that is being locked down by an Imperial Inquisitor with a warship at the dock.

You are a scavenger. You survive by knowing what you can carry and what you have to leave behind.

You turn away from the elevator and head for the exit.

choices.text: Continue

e_pre_clean.text:
In the antechamber, you saw the same channels cut into the same stone — the same light-carrying geometry. You understand, without being able to explain how, exactly where the pressure plate’s trigger point is. Not learned. Recognised.

Your hand goes to the right spot without hesitation. You press, and the plate clicks inert.

You lift the Void Compass from the pedestal. It is warm before you touch it. The silver veins pulse brighter, and for a moment you feel the same pull you felt in the antechamber — that sense of direction, pointing somewhere specific. It is stronger now. Much stronger.

choices.text: Let the compass show you what it wants to show you
